summary: BSON tests aren't run and BSON C encoder/decoder are not used when the tests are fixed
description:
Mac-Pro:mongo-ruby-driver cremes$ gem install bson_ext-1.1.1.gem 
Building native extensions.  This could take a while...
Successfully installed bson_ext-1.1.1
1 gem installed
Installing ri documentation for bson_ext-1.1.1...
Installing RDoc documentation for bson_ext-1.1.1...
Mac-Pro:mongo-ruby-driver cremes$ ruby -S irb
ruby-1.9.2-p0 > require 'bson'
 => true 
ruby-1.9.2-p0 > BSON::BSON_CODER.deserialize(BSON::BSON_CODER.serialize({}))
NoMethodError: undefined method `pack' for :BSON::ByteBuffer
	from /Users/cremes/.rvm/gems/ruby-1.9.2-p0/gems/bson-1.1.1/lib/bson/byte_buffer.rb:33:in `initialize'
	from /Users/cremes/.rvm/gems/ruby-1.9.2-p0/gems/bson-1.1.1/lib/bson/bson_c.rb:28:in `new'
	from /Users/cremes/.rvm/gems/ruby-1.9.2-p0/gems/bson-1.1.1/lib/bson/bson_c.rb:28:in `deserialize'
	from (irb):2
	from /Users/cremes/.rvm/rubies/ruby-1.9.2-p0/bin/irb:17:in `<main>'



The same simple test works with the pure ruby code paths.

Also, the Rakefile needs an update to use the new path to the bson tests since it was renamed from mongo_bson to plain bson.

Lastly, the tests don't all use MONGO_RUBY_DRIVER_PORT and MONGO_RUBY_DRIVER_HOST environment variables, so on systems where mongo is running on a non-standard port the tests fail (even when setting and exporting these vars).

summary: Problem with BSON and JRuby
description:
I am not sure if this is a jruby or bson bug, so I'll start here first... :)

When using the ruby mongo driver from JRuby in 1.9 compatibility mode the BSON module tries to change frozen strings.


# cat test.rb
require 'rubygems'
require 'bson'

h = {
    "x" => 1
}

p BSON::BSON_RUBY.serialize(h, true)

------------------

# ruby -v
ruby 1.9.1p378 (2010-01-10 revision 26273) [i386-darwin10]

#<BSON::ByteBuffer:0x00000100840df0 @buf=[12, 0, 0, 0, 16, 120, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0], @cursor=4, @order=:little_endian, @int_pack_order="V", @double_pack_order="E">

------------------

summary: GridFS: don't create indexes if slave_ok is true
Mongo::Connection.new.db('dbname', options) - options are not passed to the db initializer
description:
If options (like :pkfactory) are specified for connection.db() they are not passed to the db initalizier. Therefore it is not possible to set the options.

File "connection.rb", L295:
def db(db_name, options={})
      DB.new(db_name, self)
end

I think it should look like this:
def db(db_name, options={})
      DB.new(db_name, self, options)
end

summary: Seed order in Mongo::Connection.multi makes or breaks the result
description:
Let's say we have three nodes on replica set, each running in localhost port 30000,30001 and 30002 respectively.  I connect using

MongoMapper.connection = Mongo::Connection.multi([ ["localhost", 30000], ["localhost",30002] ])

If the last seed (localhost:30002) is not up and running,  I get

connection.rb:483:in `connect': failed to connect to any given host:port (Mongo::ConnectionFailure)

I think this is probably because http://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/blob/master/lib/mongo/connection.rb#L604 as call to close will also clean up previously selected master.

summary: Mongo driver has the side-effect of breaking the redis ruby driver
description:
Hello,

I know you could argue this is equally a redis ruby driver issue as much as a mongo issue, but none-the-less the redis ruby driver will not work if you have loaded the mongo driver as well.

For example:

Loading development environment (Rails 2.3.8)
>> require "mongo"
=> ["Mongo"]
>> include Mongo
=> Object
>> require "redis"
=> ["Redis"]
>> redis = Redis.new(:host => "localhost", :port => 6379, :db => 0)
(Object doesn't support #inspect)
=> 
>> redis.set "foo", "bar"
NoMethodError: undefined method `disconnect' for #<Mongo::Connection:0x28b97648>
    from /usr/local/lib/ruby/gems/1.9/gems/redis-2.1.1/lib/redis/client.rb:73:in `disconnect'
    from /usr/local/lib/ruby/gems/1.9/gems/redis-2.1.1/lib/redis/client.rb:162:in `rescue in ensure_connected'
    from /usr/local/lib/ruby/gems/1.9/gems/redis-2.1.1/lib/redis/client.rb:153:in `ensure_connected'
    from /usr/local/lib/ruby/gems/1.9/gems/redis-2.1.1/lib/redis/client.rb:58:in `block in process'
    from /usr/local/lib/ruby/gems/1.9/gems/redis-2.1.1/lib/redis/client.rb:118:in `logging'
    from /usr/local/lib/ruby/gems/1.9/gems/redis-2.1.1/lib/redis/client.rb:57:in `process'
    from /usr/local/lib/ruby/gems/1.9/gems/redis-2.1.1/lib/redis/client.rb:29:in `call'
    from /usr/local/lib/ruby/gems/1.9/gems/redis-2.1.1/lib/redis.rb:466:in `set'
    from (irb):5
    from /usr/local/bin/irb:12:in `<main>'

If I don't load any of the mongo stuff, everything works fine...

We are currently running:

mongo (1.1.4)
mongo_ext (0.19.3)
redis (2.1.1)

ruby 1.9.2p0 (2010-08-18 revision 29036) [i386-freebsd7]
Rails 2.3.8

Thanks....

summary: Read from passive nodes, too.
The port is set to 0 (instead of DEFAULT_PORT) if the optional port is not specified in the replica set config record
description:
?I've submitted a pull request for this issue:

https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/pull/14

Note the following config record containing hosts without the [optional](http://www.mongodb.org/display/DOCS/Replica+Set+Configuration) ":port" specified.  When a connection is made, the discovery code will set the port to 0 when the host string is split on  ":".

I also noticed that @arbiters is not being set for this config record, but I didn't attempt a fix for this.

    >db.runCommand("isMaster")
    {
	"setName" : "stage1",
	"ismaster" : true,
	"secondary" : false,
	"hosts" : [
		"mongo01.customink.com",
		"mongo02.customink.com"
	],
	"arbiters" : [
		"oa.customink.com"
	],
	"ok" : 1
    }

summary: Mongo::GridIO#seek broken after #read_all (or: GridIO#read_all breaks all future operations?)
description:
As of the Ruby mongo-1.1.4 gem, GridIO#read_all is defined as:

    # Read a file in its entirety.
    def read_all
      buf = ''
      while true
        buf << @current_chunk['data'].to_s
        @current_chunk = get_chunk(@current_chunk['n'] + 1)
        break unless @current_chunk
      end
      buf
    end

The shortcut of setting @current_chunk = get_chunk(next_n) has a side effect of breaking anything that depends on @current_chunk.  read_length handles this properly, though it may be a few more instructions than is required when doing a read_all.

WORKAROUND:
Using GridIO#read_length(file_length_or_remaining_length) doesn't break further seeks, etc.

summary: bson gem includes jruby
description:
The bson gem's .gemfile has a wildcard include for jars, which ends up including a vendored jruby jar, making the bson gem about 8MB's larger than it needs to be: https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/blob/master/bson.gemspec#L17

Also, it would be awesome if there were platform specific gems, so that the jruby dependencies would not need to be included for the "ruby" platform's gem.

thanks.

summary: Allow the use of symbols for index names with index management methods
description:
As a Ruby developer I default to symbols in most cases for identifiers and the Ruby driver accepts this is pretty much every case, but this is an exception.

Mongo::Collection#create_index :field_name, :name => :index_name
Mongo::Collection#drop_index :index_name
Mongo::DB.drop_index :collection_name, :index_name

summary: multi update finishes before changes are actually made
description:
I have a collection with a few 100k records on a replica set.

When I update all records to set :send_weekly_emails => false:

Person.collection.update({}, { '$set' => { :send_weekly_emails => false }}, { :multi => true })

Then I query (in a separate rails console) to see if things have gone through and it takes several minutes before all of the people have the flag set to false.

> Person.where(:send_weekly_emails => true).count # => 56
> Person.where(:send_weekly_emails => true).count # => 38
> Person.where(:send_weekly_emails => true).count # => 37
> Person.where(:send_weekly_emails => true).count # => 2
> Person.where(:send_weekly_emails => true).count # => 2
> Person.where(:send_weekly_emails => true).count # => 2
> Person.where(:send_weekly_emails => true).count # => 2
> Person.where(:send_weekly_emails => true).count # => 0

Isn't the multi update supposed to be atomic?  If anything, I would not expect it to finish before the changes were actually persisted to the replica set.

Note: the send_weeky_emails field is not indexed.

summary: Found a severe problem with the C extension running with MRI 1.9.2p0 on Windows *only*
description:
Inserting data using MRI 1.9.2p0 is incorrect. It doesn't fail, but viewing the data with the mongo console shows that all values are "null". Even weirder, I can mongodump the collection and bsondump to json format and the data is CORRECT even though the mongo console told me it was all null!

Comparing the output of bsondump in debug mode shows that something is very wrong.

The following gist shows that this works okay with MRI 1.9.1p430 and *fails* with 1.9.2p0.

http://gist.github.com/618491

I confirmed this is related to the C extension. Removing the C extension and doing a pure ruby insert from 1.9.2p0 works as expected. 

(When viewing through mongo console, the pure ruby insert shows the number directly. Using MRI 1.9.1p430 and the C extension lists all integers as NumberLong( x ), so the C extension that *does* work has slightly different behavior than the C extension on linux/osx.)

I was only able to reproduce this on Windows; I could *not* reproduce on UNIX.

summary: client driver loses synchronization with comunication stream due to exceptions at certain points
description:
The ruby client driver loses track of its position in the stream of responses from the server if an exception occurs while Connection#send_with_safe_check or Connection#receive_message are running. This commonly occurs when the client application uses a Timeout; a Timeout::Error exception may be raised while Connection#receive is blocked. Other exceptions are possible here (due to other asynchronous signals or perhaps due to unexpected IO conditions). When an exception occurs at this point, the server will eventually send a response, but the client driver is no longer waiting for it. The response is typically consumed by the subsequent mongo operations; all following request appear to get the response from the preceding request. 

(Unless there is a sequence number in the mongo protocol that is being ignored by the client driver...) I'd advise adding an exception handler to the critical section of Connection#receive_message and Connection#send_with_safe_check that closes the socket when any exception occurs. (See attached patch)

summary: URI parsing issues with passwords that include dots in version 1.1.5+ of the Ruby driver
description:
I can no longer connect to my MongoHQ database using passwords with 
dots in them after upgrading to Mongoid 2.0.0.rc1, with the 1.1.5 
version of the MongoDB Ruby driver. The issue is the 
MONGODB_URI_MATCHER in 'lib/mongo/util/uri_parser.rb'. 

I've tested this both on my local dev machine and on two Heroku instances. Here is the URI (changed user and pass, but kept the same format):
 
mongodb://username:AB.1234@arrow.mongohq.com:27081/appname-staging

Here is the stack trace from one of my Heroku instances (which reflects the error on my local dev machine):

\d]+))?(\/([-\d\w]+))?/ 

It resolved my issue. I can't test this out on Heroku.

summary: Pooling doesn't seem to handle authentication.
description: If :pool_size is declared in the options with a value larger than 1, the authentication applies only to the first socket. There are no obvious ways to work-around the issue and the unit tests also seem to lack a combination of pool_size over 1 used with authentication. Pools work just fine, if mongod is started without --auth.

require 'rubygems'

summary: Documentation that encourages improper replica setup
description:
Replica sets that have two members do not fail over properly because if a member goes down, a new master cannot be elected.

repl_set_connection.rb has examples of two member replica sets in it's documentation.

    # @example Connect to a replica set and provide two seed nodes:
    #   ReplSetConnection.new(['localhost', 30000], ['localhost', 30001])
    #
    # @example Connect to a replica set providing two seed nodes and ensuring a connection to the replica set named 'prod':
    #   ReplSetConnection.new(['localhost', 30000], ['localhost', 30001], :rs_name => 'prod')
    #
    # @example Connect to a replica set providing two seed nodes and allowing reads from a secondary node:
    #   ReplSetConnection.new(['localhost', 30000], ['localhost', 30001], :read_secondary => true)

This is an unsafe setup. Please provide a safe setup and note it in the docs.

summary: Mongo connections/operations become extremely slow when a secondary node is taken down
description:
I had a 5 member replica set. A secondary node was removed. A new master was very quickly elected. Queries to mongo got extremely slow.

I initialized the ReplSetConnection like so:

::Mongo::ReplSetConnection.new(["ip1", 27017], ["ip2", 27017], ["ip3", 27017], ["ip4", 27017], ["ip5", 27017], :logger => Rails.logger)

When I ran rs.conf() on the mongo command console, the secondary server that was taken down remained in the list.

I was told there must be an odd number of nodes, so I removed the master node and used the instructions at http://www.mongodb.org/display/DOCS/Reconfiguring+when+Members+are+Up to reconfigure the set.

When the two dead nodes were removed, the connection was fast again.

summary: Alias Cursor#next for Cursor#next_document
#find_and_modify Error Handling Problems
description:
This issue consists of actually two closely related problems.

1) The #find_and_modify does not support the "safe" option, hence there's no way to make find_and_modify raise an exception when an error is encountered. This also makes it inconsistent with the rest of the find, modification and insert methods.
2) The #find_and_modify method, when used with the "new" option, causes get_last_error to always report success, even if the update fails. My theory is that get_last_error only works for the last operation in #find_and_modify, so either the "find" if it happens last, or the "update", if it happens last. When the "new" option is given, this obviously causes the "find" operation to happen last, hence any error that occurred during the update operation is overwritten and lost.

summary: Responses sometimes out of order on Ruby 1.9.2
UTF-8 issue with 1.9 str = "123\xD9"
description:
str = "123\xD9"

This non-utf8 string can serialized properly on 1.9 even though it shouldn't.

summary: Add StringIO methods to GridIO
description:
For instance:

module Mongo
  class GridIO
    def eof?
      @file_position >= @file_length
    end
  end
end

summary: Re-add Connection#host and Connection#port
Automatically create a new socket on fork
description:
This will keep people from having to manage reconnects on their own:

https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-python-driver/blob/master/pymongo/connection.py#L174-193

Might want to check for performance issues. Also, Ruby's Process.pid doesn't work on all platforms. Need to deal with that, too.
